title: 80% to 24%: what happened to HCP access and why it matters for your business
description: The data behind the collapse. What it means practically for a sales team trying to hit target.

title: The most expensive belief in healthcare sales: "persistence pays off."
description: When access was 80%, persistence worked. At 24%, it's just expensive. The maths of the model.

title: Your sales team isn't failing. The model is failing
description: Reframe from people problem to systems problem.

title: The real cost of a rep visit: why the maths no longer works
description: Fully loaded cost per meaningful meeting in healthcare field sales: £100-300. Most med device and diagnostics companies haven't done this calculation. Anchor in hospital visits, surgeon access, NHS trust coverage.

title: What happens when your best channel stops working
description: The healthcare sales-led model (rep visits to hospitals, surgeon meetings, clinical demonstrations) worked for decades. Then HCP access collapsed and the environment changed. Most med device companies are still running the old playbook.

title: Most healthcare companies have a demand problem disguised as a sales problem
description: The central thesis. The reason companies hire more reps instead of fixing the real issue.

title: The three signs you have a demand problem, not a sales problem
description: Practical diagnostic. If your reps struggle to book meetings, if your pipeline is thin at the top, if new hires don't change the numbers.

title: Why "hire more reps" is the most common and most expensive wrong answer in healthcare
description: The instinct when procedure volumes are flat and surgeon access is declining. Why adding more field reps to cover NHS trusts doesn't work when the real problem is demand.

title: The 73% problem: why most healthcare launches miss forecast
description: The stat, the pattern, and the systemic causes. Not bad products. Not bad people. Bad systems.

title: Diagnosing a stalled pipeline: is it sales or demand?
description: Practical framework for healthcare commercial leaders to work out which problem they actually have. Anchor in surgeon evaluations stalling, procurement cycles lengthening, clinical champions going quiet.

title: The cost of another quarter without demand generation
description: Quantifying what inaction looks like for a med device or diagnostics company. Pipeline of surgeon evaluations decaying, field reps leaving, launch windows missed while competitors build visibility with HCPs.

title: Your competitors aren't waiting. The market doesn't pause while you figure this out
description: Urgency without panic. The window for first-mover advantage in positioning within a surgical category or therapy area. While you're debating, a competitor is building visibility with the surgeons and procurement leads who'll decide next year's contracts.

title: The company that missed its window: what happens when you're second to market but last to mind
description: A med device company had a better product but arrived after a competitor had already built relationships with the key surgeons and clinical champions.

title: Why good products fail: the distance between clinical excellence and commercial success
description: Clinical evidence is necessary but not sufficient. The gap is commercial execution.

title: "The product sells itself" is the most expensive assumption in healthcare
description: It never sells itself. The products that win are the ones with the best go-to-market, not always the best clinical data.

title: What your launch plan is probably missing
description: Most launch plans focus on the product. The missing piece is the system around it.

title: Second to market but first to mind: how positioning beats timing
description: You don't have to be first. You have to be best positioned.

title: The launch that worked by accident (and what it teaches us about systems)
description: The short stem story. Marketing and sales accidentally aligned across every touchpoint. Result: £2-3 million above forecast.

title: The company that targeted 90 specialties and got it wrong
description: The Swedish company story. Home market assumptions don't translate. Local research matters.

title: When your best rep outsells your worst by 3:1, that's not a performance gap. It's a systems gap
description: The playbook argument.

title: The playbook your sales team will actually use (vs the one gathering dust)
description: What makes a playbook usable vs theoretical.

title: Messaging that lands in 30 seconds: why your elevator pitch needs work
description: The 30-second test. If a rep can't explain the value in 30 seconds, the messaging architecture is wrong.

title: What your best rep knows that nobody else does
description: The knowledge trapped in one person's head. The cost of not capturing it.

title: The 5 P's of sales enablement: Playbooks, Pitch tools, Products for prospects, Propositions for C-suite, Proposals that win tenders
description: Framework overview.

title: The tender process should start six months before the deadline. Most companies start six weeks before
description: Multi-million pound opportunities handled with the wrong template.

title: The messaging test: can a new hire deliver your pitch after one read?
description: If they can't, it's not a training problem. It's a messaging problem.

title: Why your proposals look like everyone else's (and what to do about it)
description: The procurement director sees ten proposals. Nine look identical.

title: Building a pitch that works for every stakeholder in the decision-making web
description: Surgeon cares about outcomes. Procurement cares about cost. Finance cares about ROI. One pitch doesn't fit all.

title: Your website is either generating demand or just existing. Which is it?
description: The digital presence test. Is your website a brochure or a demand engine?

title: Being found for problems, not just product names
description: SEO strategy for healthcare. The people who need your product are searching for solutions to problems, not your brand name.

title: Content that warms prospects before the first meeting
description: The shift from cold outreach to warm conversations. What happens when a prospect has already consumed your content before the rep calls.

title: 77% of buyers research before engaging sales. What are they finding about you?
description: The digital first impression.

title: The best salespeople in healthcare are clinicians who love your product
description: The restaurant conversation. Two surgeons discussing a device. No rep needed.

title: You can't meet everyone. Your advocates can
description: Why advocate programmes scale reach beyond sales team capacity.

title: How to build a KOL programme that isn't just a dinner
description: The difference between entertaining KOLs and activating them.

title: The annual course that created centres of excellence
description: From the short stem story. Design surgeons teaching juniors. Emotional bonds. Reference sites.

title: 50 sets for 100 people: creating urgency ethically
description: The J&J launch. Scarcity as a launch tool.

title: You've got CE marking. Now what?
description: For startups who've done the hard technical work but haven't thought about commercial execution.

title: The founder-led sales ceiling: when your growth depends on one person being in every meeting
description: What happens when the founder is the only person who can sell the product.

title: New market entry: why your home market playbook won't work here
description: The Swedish company lesson applied more broadly.

title: First customers to first system: when to stop selling and start building
description: The transition from founder hustle to repeatable process.

title: 24 minutes per week. That's all you get
description: The attention reality. Where HCP time actually goes. What makes something worth their time.

title: Where the time actually goes: mapping an HCP's week
description: Practical breakdown of the 24-minute window. Clinical commitments, admin, personal time, and where supplier interactions fit.

title: Why HCPs stopped taking meetings
description: It's not hostility. It's time pressure and information availability. They can find what they need without you.

title: The rep's day: driving around looking for people to help
description: The reality of sales before demand exists. Convinced the products could help but no one knew you were coming.

title: Your biggest competitor isn't the company with a similar product
description: It's everything else competing for the same 24 minutes. Conferences, journals, email, colleagues, lunch.

title: The information your rep used to carry now lives on LinkedIn
description: What happens when HCPs can access everything your rep was going to tell them without booking a meeting.

title: Competing for attention when everyone claims to be innovative
description: The word "innovative" means nothing when everyone uses it. What actually earns attention.

title: Why the old tribes don't work anymore: how data replaced belief systems in healthcare
description: Clinicians used to align with philosophies. Now registries show what works. The argument shifted from belief to evidence.

title: The difference between content that exists and content that earns attention
description: Most healthcare content is published and forgotten. What makes the difference.

title: What healthcare professionals actually read, watch, and share
description: Based on observation, not assumption. The formats that get engagement vs the formats companies keep producing.

title: Content that warms vs content that wastes
description: The test: does this content make the next conversation easier? If not, why are you producing it?

title: The scroll test: would someone who lands on your profile think "I need to talk to this person"?
description: Applied to company profiles, not just personal.

title: The single clinician buyer is gone
description: Decision-making in healthcare now involves procurement, finance, clinical governance, patient groups, trust boards.

title: The stakeholder map your sales team doesn't have
description: Most reps know the surgeon. They don't know the procurement lead, the finance director, or the clinical governance committee member who has veto power.

title: How decisions really get made in healthcare institutions
description: The formal process vs the real process. Committee meetings, corridor conversations, and the person who didn't attend but whose opinion matters most.

title: The difference between being known and being chosen
description: Awareness isn't selection. The gap between "I've heard of them" and "they're on the shortlist."

title: The revenue lever most healthcare companies don't know they have
description: Traditional model: sell to clinician, clinician uses product, patient receives product. The missing step: help clinics attract the patients who need their services.

title: Patient marketing isn't off-limits. You just don't know how
description: The biggest misconception in healthcare marketing. Most companies assume patient marketing is prohibited. It's not. It needs the right process.

title: 10% year-on-year growth in UK self-pay. What this means for healthcare companies
description: The market is there. Patients are searching. Most companies aren't reaching them.

title: 77% of patients research treatments online. What they're finding is usually nothing useful
description: The gap between patient demand for information and what healthcare companies are providing.

title: Viagra didn't wait for doctors to prescribe it. It created patient demand
description: The original patient-pull model. TV advertising created demand that pulled through the healthcare system.

title: The document that gets your legal team to say yes
description: Not about understanding ABPI codes. About having the right process document that addresses legal's concerns before they arise.

title: What ABPI, ASA, and MHRA actually say vs what people assume
description: The gap between what regulations require and what compliance teams assume they require.

title: "We can't do that" usually means "we don't know how."
description: The most common barrier to patient marketing isn't regulations. It's assumption.

title: Working with your legal team, not around them
description: The claims matrix approach. Building evidence-linked claims that legal can approve confidently.

title: The cost of not marketing to patients: who loses?
description: When patients don't find treatments that could help them, everyone loses. The company, the clinic, and the patient.

title: You sell to the clinic. The clinic needs patients. You help the clinic attract patients. Everyone wins
description: The co-marketing model explained simply.

title: Turning your customers into patient acquisition partners
description: The commercial relationship shifts from supplier to partner when you help clinics grow.

title: The co-marketing model that makes your product harder to replace
description: When you're generating patients for a clinic, switching to a competitor becomes much harder. Competitive moat through patient volume.

title: 34% increase in direct patient enquiries: what happened when a clinic stopped depending on consultant referrals
description: Case study proof.

title: Why most patient materials fail: what Bath research tells us about how patients read health information
description: Partnership with Professor Christopher Eccleston. The science behind patient communication.

title: The weight of words: why language matters more in patient communication than anywhere else in healthcare marketing
description: From the Painful Conversations series.

title: What motivates patients to act (and what stops them)
description: The barriers between knowing about a treatment and actually seeking it.

title: Patient content that drives action vs patient content that just exists
description: The difference between a patient information leaflet and a patient engagement strategy.

title: Define success before committing budget. What are you actually trying to achieve at this event?
description: Most event planning starts with stand design. It should start with success criteria.

title: The pre-event strategy that determines 80% of your results
description: What you do before the event matters more than what happens during it. Target accounts, meeting strategy, success criteria.

title: "We need to be at [major congress]" is not a strategy
description: The most common justification for event spend. And the weakest.

title: Healthcare marketing spend dropped from 9.6% to 7.2% of revenue. CFOs are demanding ROI
description: The budget pressure context. Why "it was worth it for the visibility" no longer passes.

title: A badge scan is not a lead. It's a name attached to someone who stopped for free coffee
description: The core Beyond the Badge Scan argument.

title: 400 badges, 7 leads: the event that proved badge scans are worthless
description: Real numbers from a real event. The gap between scan count and actual pipeline.

title: Decision-maker density: the metric your events team isn't tracking
description: What percentage of your booth conversations were with people who can actually buy?

title: What "good energy at the stand" actually means (nothing)
description: The subjective assessment that replaces real measurement.

title: The follow-up process that turns event contacts into conversations
description: What happens after the event matters as much as what happens during it. Most follow-up is generic email blasts.

title: Booth traffic is not pipeline
description: The distinction between volume and value. One decision-maker is worth 100 students.

title: Most companies attend too many events badly rather than fewer events well
description: The portfolio problem. Spreading budget thin across six events instead of dominating two.

title: How to know which events to attend and which to cut
description: Practical framework for evaluating event ROI and making portfolio decisions.

title: The event your competitors are ignoring (and why you should be there)
description: Sometimes the best events aren't the biggest congresses. They're the smaller gatherings where decision-makers are more accessible.

title: What good event ROI measurement actually looks like
description: Moving from "we had good conversations" to pipeline attribution, cost per qualified conversation, and investment-to-pipeline ratio.

title: 340% ROI demonstrated: what changed when one company started measuring what matters
description: Case study. Global MedTech subsidiary. Marketing earned increased budget allocation by proving event value.

title: In a world where everything's recorded, give people a reason to be in the room
description: Why "content" isn't enough if it can be consumed later. Engagement design.

title: Designing event content people can't get from the recording
description: Dialogue over monologue. Interaction over presentation. Why your congress booth should feel different from your website.

title: The Daytona USA stand: when engagement beats strategy (and when it doesn't)
description: The story of an impressive stand that generated attention but not pipeline.

title: 40 stands at a congress, all competing for the same audience. Why would anyone stop at yours?
description: The differentiation question applied to events.

title: You probably can't afford the big congresses. Here's what to do instead
description: For startups. Smaller events, advisory boards, targeted engagement with named accounts.

title: Why your value proposition sounds like everyone else's
description: The differentiation test. If you swap your company name for a competitor's, does the value proposition still work?

title: The gap between what your product does and what your audience needs to hear
description: Clinical features vs commercial outcomes. Most companies lead with what they're proud of, not what the buyer cares about.

title: "Clinically proven" means nothing to a procurement director
description: Different stakeholders need different messages. What sounds convincing to a surgeon is noise to a finance team.

title: Turning features into outcomes that matter to each stakeholder
description: The practical exercise: for each feature, ask "so what?" until you reach the outcome that matters to this specific person.

title: Why your messaging feels flat: the most common cause
description: Usually it's not bad writing. It's that the messaging was built from the inside out (what we want to say) instead of the outside in (what they need to hear).

title: Brand messaging, product messaging, sales messaging: why you need all three
description: The three layers framework. Most companies have one (badly).

title: What happens when your only messaging is product messaging
description: Brand messaging creates context. Sales messaging creates action. Product messaging alone does neither effectively.

title: The messaging architecture that makes every touchpoint consistent
description: From website to pitch deck to conference stand to patient leaflet. One architecture, multiple expressions.

title: The hierarchy of messaging: what comes first and why sequence matters
description: You can't build sales messaging without product messaging. You can't build product messaging without brand messaging. The sequence is non-negotiable.

title: How to build a claims matrix that legal actually approves
description: The document that connects every claim to its evidence. What gets approved and what gets killed.

title: The approval workflow that stops good ideas dying in review
description: Not about understanding regulations. About building the process that lets creative work get through.

title: "MLR will kill any good creative" is what people say when they don't know how to brief legal properly
description: Reframe from barrier to process.

title: Evidence maps: linking every claim to proof before legal ever sees it
description: Proactive compliance. Don't submit and hope. Build the evidence trail first.

title: The sales playbook framework: not a PDF that sits in a drawer
description: What makes a playbook a living tool vs a document nobody opens.

title: The playbook nobody uses vs the playbook everyone uses: what's different
description: Practical differences in format, length, accessibility, and how it's introduced to the team.

title: Capturing what your best rep knows before they leave
description: The knowledge risk. Your best performer's approach is in their head. What happens when they move on?

title: If I was a commercial director building a sales playbook for the first time
description: Tactical walkthrough of the process.

title: The objection handling section nobody writes (and everyone needs)
description: Most playbooks skip objection handling or treat it as an afterthought. It's the section reps use most.

title: Your pitch has 30 seconds. What are you doing with them?
description: The founder pitch challenge. You know everything about your product. You need to communicate the value in half a minute.

title: Positioning before product: why startups need messaging before they need marketing
description: Getting the story right before spending money on channels.

title: The investor pitch vs the customer pitch: why they're different and most founders use one for both
description: Investors buy potential. Customers buy outcomes. The messaging is fundamentally different.

title: The attention economy in healthcare: why everything has changed
description: Overview piece connecting access collapse, decision-making web, information overload.

title: If I was a marketing director who'd just been told "your HCPs won't take meetings."
description: Practical tactical response.

title: The channels that used to work and the channels that work now
description: What's changed, what's stayed, and what most companies haven't noticed.

title: Reaching patients compliantly: the access problem nobody's solved
description: Combining patient marketing and attention economy themes.

title: If I was a product manager who'd just received brilliant clinical data and needed to turn it into a commercial story
The Portuguese man who saw our product for the first time
description: The recognition problem in action. A product that deserved recognition but nobody knew about.

title: Why most healthcare companies sell features when they should sell outcomes
description: The fundamental messaging error.

title: That feeling: leaving a hospital knowing you've made a difference
description: The emotional core of why healthcare marketing matters. Why helping innovations reach people who need them is important work.

title: In most therapeutic areas, the best product doesn't win. The best positioned product wins
description: The harsh truth about differentiation.

title: What "differentiation" actually means when your clinical data is similar to three competitors
description: Real-world positioning challenge.

title: The Glasgow Wonka Experience: what happens when you overpromise and underdeliver
description: Trust destruction in one event. The healthcare parallel: don't claim what you can't evidence.

title: Patient marketing as a competitive moat: the thing your competitors probably aren't doing
description: Patient marketing itself is the differentiator.

title: "Why not just hire a full-service agency?"
description: Because you'll be one of 40 clients. And they'll sell you what they sell, not what you need.

title: "Can't my marketing team do this?"
description: Possibly. But do they have 20 years of healthcare experience across 47 launches? And will they stay?

title: "We don't have the budget for an agency."
description: What's the cost of another quarter without demand generation? Let's model it.

title: "We tried marketing before and it didn't work."
description: Was it demand creation or was it tactics without strategy? There's a difference.

title: If I was a marketing manager in MedTech with a £50K budget and no playbook
If I was a CEO frustrated with marketing spend but unsure what to change
If I was a commercial director at a startup with CE marking and no sales team
If I was preparing for my first major congress with a 3x3 stand and two people
If I was a product manager who'd just been told to "do something about patient marketing."
If I was a marketing director measured on MQLs but suspecting they're the wrong metric
If I was a sales director watching my team struggle to book meetings
If I was new in role and had to present a marketing strategy to the board in 90 days
The careers test: how I ended up exactly where I was supposed to be
description: Founder Friday. The path that only makes sense looking backwards.

title: All because of a lunch allowance and a Golf
description: Founder Friday. Pragmatic decisions that change everything.

title: The surgeon who saved my finger and the product I sold him twenty years later
description: Tuesday Story / emotional content. Healthcare connections come full circle.

title: Freedom vs belonging: the trade-off nobody warns you about
description: Founder Friday. What you gain and what you give up.

title: Two versions of me exist. LinkedIn me and 11pm me
description: Founder Friday. Authenticity and the gap between perception and reality.

title: The colleagues I miss most aren't the ones I expected
description: Founder Friday. The loneliness of running something alone.

title: Shanghai: what demand generation at scale looks like
description: Tuesday Story. The conference where everything worked.

title: The golf caddy that disappeared: how information killed a healthcare role
description: Tuesday Story / market evolution. When reps carried information HCPs couldn't get elsewhere. That role is gone.

title: 300% price markups between hospitals five miles apart: how transparency changed healthcare
description: Market evolution content. The old world of information asymmetry is gone.

title: Stop moaning about the status quo. Be unignorable
description: There is a whole category of LinkedIn healthcare content that complains about how hard it is to get through to clinicians, how procurement is broken, how the system resists change. It gets likes. It changes nothing. The alternative: demonstrate value so brilliantly at every step that your product becomes impossible to ignore. Healthcare's high threshold for change keeps patients safe. Clinicians should have high standards. Your job is to meet them.

title: The LinkedIn consensus trap: why agreeing with the crowd is the opposite of standing out
description: Posts that complain about healthcare's resistance to change perform well because everyone recognises the frustration. But recognition is not commercial traction. If your content strategy is "say what everyone already thinks," you're building an audience that nods along but never buys.

title: Healthcare's high threshold for change is a feature, not a bug
description: Everyone complains about how hard it is to get healthcare to adopt new technology. They're missing the point. That resistance protects patients. A clinician who demands evidence before changing practice is doing their job. Your job is to make the evidence so compelling and the value so obvious that adoption becomes the rational choice.

title: If I was told "clinicians just won't change," here's what I'd actually do
description: Stop fighting the system. Start working with it. Map the decision-making process. Identify the evidence threshold. Build the case step by step. Make it easy to say yes. The companies that succeed in healthcare don't overcome resistance, they make resistance unnecessary.

title: The rep echo chamber: when your sales team only talks about your product
description: Most reps spend their days in broadcast mode. Product features, clinical data, competitive advantages. They know everything about what they sell and increasingly little about who they sell to. When you spend all your time transmitting, you stop receiving. You forget what problems you solve. You forget how you make your customers' day better.

title: Your rep knows the product. Do they know the customer's Tuesday afternoon?
description: The difference between product knowledge and customer knowledge. Most reps can recite a feature list. Few can describe what their customer's working day actually looks like, what pressures they face at 3pm on a Tuesday, what would make their week easier. That gap is where lost deals live.

title: Broadcast mode is the most expensive habit in healthcare sales
description: When reps default to transmitting instead of listening, every meeting becomes a product demo that nobody asked for. The customer is polite. The rep feels productive. Nothing moves forward. The cost isn't just the meeting. It's the intelligence you didn't gather, the problem you didn't hear, and the relationship that stayed transactional.

title: Demonstrating value is not a presentation. It's a conversation
description: Holistic, customer-centric value demonstration means understanding the whole picture: the clinical need, the operational constraint, the budget pressure, the political dynamic. You can't demonstrate value in broadcast mode because you don't know what valuable looks like to them.
